planting.note: Plant small cuttings or divisions at any time in very well-drained, dry, slightly alkaline to neutral, rocky, sandy, or poor soil in full sun to partial shade; biting stonecrop is extremely easy to establish from cuttings or stem fragments, rooting wherever they contact soil. Perfect for rock walls, dry slopes, and green roofs.

directions: Plant cuttings or division fragments in any well-drained, dry, slightly alkaline to neutral, rocky, sandy, or lean soil in full sun to partial shade; biting stonecrop is one of the most widely distributed and adaptable low succulent groundcovers in the world — a tiny, mat-forming Eurasian sedum naturalized throughout North America on rocky outcrops, walls, roofs, and disturbed dry ground. The dense mats of tiny, cylindrical, tightly-packed, fleshy leaves turn bright yellow-green with masses of brilliant yellow, star-shaped flowers in early summer. The name 'biting' refers to the sharp, burning taste of the leaves (due to alkaloids) — the plant is slightly toxic and should not be eaten. 'Wall pepper' comes from its habit of growing on old stone and brick walls throughout Europe. Widely used for green roofs (Sedum roofs), rock gardens, rock walls, dry slopes, and paths where few other plants survive. Spreads readily from fragments — a tiny piece rooting anywhere it contacts dry soil.

planting.note: Plant cuttings or divisions in spring in very well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, dry to average, rocky or sandy, lean soil in full sun to partial shade; Caucasian stonecrop is highly adaptable and drought tolerant, spreading by creeping stems that root at the nodes to quickly cover dry, rocky, or sandy ground.

directions: Plant cuttings or divisions in spring in very well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, dry to average, rocky or sandy, lean soil in full sun to partial shade; Caucasian stonecrop (Dragon's Blood stonecrop in its red-leaved cultivars) is one of the most widely used low succulent groundcovers in temperate gardens — a mat-forming Eurasian sedum with flat, slightly scalloped, fleshy leaves that may be dark green or burgundy-red depending on cultivar, and flat-topped clusters of rose-pink to bright pink flowers in midsummer. The cultivar 'Dragon's Blood' with its deep purplish-red foliage is one of the best-selling groundcover plants in the nursery trade. 'Tricolor' has green, pink, and white variegated leaves. Spreads quickly by creeping stems rooting at nodes to cover slopes, rock walls, and dry banks. Hardy, reliable, and virtually pest-free. One of the most important species for green roof plantings, slope stabilization, and dry, hot, sunny groundcover situations where conventional plants fail.

planting.note: Plant individual rosettes or clusters in spring in very well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, rocky or sandy, lean soil in full sun; cobweb houseleek grows naturally on limestone and granite rock faces and cliffs of the Alps and Pyrenees, requiring excellent drainage and lean soils — excess fertility causes lush, open growth that loses the distinctive cobweb effect.

directions: Plant individual rosettes or clusters in spring in very well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, rocky or sandy, lean soil in full sun; cobweb houseleek is the most distinctive and charming of all the houseleeks — the small, tight rosettes of succulent leaves are covered in a characteristic network of fine, white, silky threads stretched across the rosette tips, resembling a tiny spider's web or a miniature snowflake. The species name 'arachnoideum' (spider-like) perfectly captures this remarkable feature. In midsummer, bright rose-pink, starry flowers are produced on short stems above the foliage. The cobwebbing is most pronounced and dense in full sun and lean soil conditions — excess moisture or shade causes it to lose its characteristic appearance. Multiplies readily by offsets into growing colonies. A favorite for troughs, rock gardens, dry stone walls, and hypertufa planters where its scale and intricate detail can be appreciated close up.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in moist to wet, slightly acidic, average to fertile soil in full sun to partial shade; tussock sedge grows naturally in swamps, wet meadows, and the shallow margins of ponds and streams, where it forms the characteristic elevated pedestals (tussocks) that are an important ecological feature of northeastern wetlands.

directions: Plant divisions in spring in moist to wet, slightly acidic, average to fertile soil in full sun to partial shade; tussock sedge is the most ecologically important and structurally distinctive native sedge of northeastern North American wetlands — forming the characteristic large, elevated 'tussocks' (raised, columnar mounds of tightly packed dead and living stems) that are one of the most recognizable features of northeastern sedge meadows, swamps, and wet prairies. The tussocks serve as elevated nesting and resting platforms for many wetland birds and small mammals, and as elevated refuges from standing water. Blue-wing teal, sedge wrens, and least bitterns nest among tussock sedge clumps. Old established tussocks can reach 3–4 feet in height and diameter after decades of growth. An essential species for ecological wetland plantings, bioswales, rain gardens with wet zones, and naturalistic wet-meadow restorations.

planting.note: Plant divisions or container-grown plants in spring in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade; Hosta hypoleuca produces larger leaves than most species hostas and is a robust grower once established, forming handsome large clumps in appropriate shade conditions.

directions: Plant divisions in spring in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade; Hosta hypoleuca is a Japanese species hosta named for its most distinctive feature — the thick, waxy white powder (glaucous bloom) coating the underside of each leaf, creating a dramatic bicolor effect when the large, dark gray-green upper leaf surface is contrasted with the brilliant white underside. Native to rocky cliffs and shaded slopes in central Honshu, Japan. The large, ovate to cordate leaves (up to 12 inches long) and the white-powdered undersides set it apart from all other hosta species. The pale lavender to white flowers are attractive in late summer. A valuable addition to shade gardens where distinctive leaf texture and detail are important design elements. Combines beautifully with ferns and other shade plants where the white leaf undersides catch filtered light and create visual depth in the shaded border.
